title: Trump's 'Golden Dome' would need to do what no US system has ever done — and the CBO says it won't — 2026-05-13
narrative: Trump's 'Golden Dome' missile defence system has been costed by the Congressional Budget Office at $1.2 trillion — nearly seven times the initial estimate. The CBO also found the system as described 'might not stop an all-out missile attack.' Congressional Republicans are pushing for expedited funding. The White House framing: America needs this shield, and it needs it now.
telemetry: GMD INTERCEPT RATE: Ground-based Midcourse Defense (GMD) — the only US ICBM interceptor currently deployed — has a documented approximately 50% intercept rate per unclassified Missile Defense Agency public test data; this is the system the Golden Dome would build on, not replace

MULTIPLE THREAT VECTORS: The stated capability requires simultaneous interception of ICBMs, hypersonics, and cruise missiles — no existing US system can do this; each threat category requires different sensor architecture and interceptor type, none of which are currently integrated

CBO VS. WHITE HOUSE COST DELTA: The CBO's $1.2T estimate vs. the White House's claimed lower figure — the methodology gap is the story; CBO uses independent congressional scoring rules, the White House figure appears to use MDA internal projections that have consistently underestimated costs

ARROW / IRON BEAM PARALLEL: Israeli Arrow system has been in development for over 20 years and is not yet a complete operational defense; citing it as a 'groundwork' proof-of-concept for a continental US shield is a significant engineering leap without demonstrated capability

MDA OWN TIMELINES: The Missile Defense Agency's unclassified budget requests and congressional testimony provide published roadmap milestones — these show a development timeline to operational capability that is measured in decades, not the political timeline being proposed
analysis: The physical law problem is straightforward: no system currently exists that can intercept hypersonics, ICBMs, and cruise missiles in a single integrated architecture. GMD — the existing ICBM system — has a 50% test intercept rate, meaning half of incoming warheads get through under controlled test conditions. The CBO's $1.2T estimate vs. the White House's initial lower estimate reflects a methodology difference that is itself a signal: independent congressional scoring has consistently been higher than agency projections on missile defense programs. The 20-year development arc of the Israeli Arrow program, which isn't even functionally equivalent, is the realistic reference class for this kind of system. Contractors publicly saying they need 20 years, an agency with a documented 50% intercept rate, and a political timeline of 'as soon as possible' — the gap between those three things is $1.2 trillion of unfunded risk.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT] The CBO says it might not work. The MDA's own test data shows a 50% intercept rate on the only system it's based on. Contractors say 20 years. The President says America needs it now. Three of those four facts can be simultaneously true — and the fourth is a political statement, not an engineering one.
